text: The greatest thing in the world is to know how to belong to oneself.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 39 ("Of Solitude"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: To philosophize is to learn to die.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 20 ("That to Philosophise is to Learn to Die"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Not being able to govern events, I govern myself; and if they will not adapt to me, I adapt to them.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 8 ("On the Art of Conversation"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: My life has been full of terrible misfortunes, most of which never happened.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 4 ("Of Diversion"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: He who fears he shall suffer already suffers what he fears.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 13 ("Of Experience"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Every man carries the whole stamp of the human condition within him.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 2 ("Of Repentance"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: I study myself more than any other subject; it is my metaphysics, it is my physics.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 13 ("Of Experience"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: We can be knowledgeable with other men's knowledge, but we cannot be wise with other men's wisdom.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 25 ("Of the Education of Children"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: It is not death that I fear, but dying—the process of dissolution, the loss of what I have been. Death, once arrived, concerns me not at all.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 20 ("That to Philosophise is to Learn to Die"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: On the highest throne in the world, we are still sitting only on our own bottom.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 13 ("Of Experience"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: I want death to find me planting my cabbages, caring neither much nor little for it—but much less for my unfinished garden.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 20 ("That to Philosophise is to Learn to Die"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: A man who fears suffering is already suffering from what he fears. Let us learn to stand firm, and to fight fortune.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 40 ("A Consideration Upon Cicero"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Those who have compared life to a dream were right. We are sleeping wakers and waking sleepers. We do not know what things are, but only what they seem to us.
source: Essays, Book II, Ch. 12 ("Apology for Raymond Sebond"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: There is no passion so contagious as that of fear.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 18 ("Of Fear"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: We only labour to stuff the memory, and leave the conscience and the understanding unfurnished and void.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 25 ("Of the Education of Children"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: How many things served us yesterday as articles of faith, which today are fables to us? How many things appear probable to us today which perhaps will appear false tomorrow?
source: Essays, Book II, Ch. 12 ("Apology for Raymond Sebond"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: There are some defeats more triumphant than victories.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 30 ("Of Cannibals"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: I have never seen a greater monster or miracle in the world than myself.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 11 ("Of Cripples"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Men are tormented by their opinions of things, not by the things themselves.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 14 ("That the Taste of Good and Evil Depends on Opinion"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Nothing is so firmly believed as that which we least know.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 31 ("Of a Monstrous Child"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: My reason is not obliged to bow and submit; it is my knees that must.
source: Essays, Book II, Ch. 12 ("Apology for Raymond Sebond"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Every man bears the whole stamp of the human condition. Every fault you see in another, look for it in yourself.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 2 ("Of Repentance"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: The soul that harbours philosophy ought to be well-practiced in sorrow by her sanity of judgment, so that she may without surprise be set to bear the heaviest afflictions.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 20 ("That to Philosophise is to Learn to Die"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Let us give Fortune no more power over us than is necessary; let us win back what she has usurped and keep it safe from her.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 39 ("Of Solitude"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: The most certain sign of wisdom is a constant cheerfulness. Her state is like that of things above the moon, always clear and serene.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 26 ("Of Pedantry"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: To compose our character is our duty, not to compose books, and to win not battles and provinces, but order and tranquility in our conduct.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 13 ("Of Experience"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Every man is a good judge of his own interest; but no man is a good judge of his own deserts.
source: Essays, Book II, Ch. 16 ("Of Glory"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: Lend yourself to others but give yourself only to yourself.
source: Essays, Book III, Ch. 10 ("Of Husbanding the Will"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: If you don't know how to die, don't worry; Nature will tell you what to do on the spot, fully and sufficiently. She will do the job for you; don't bother your head about it.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 20 ("That to Philosophise is to Learn to Die"), trans. Charles Cotton

text: It is not reason but custom and habit that governs most of human life. The wise man therefore works on his habits first.
source: Essays, Book I, Ch. 23 ("Of Custom"), trans. Charles Cotton
